title: LockNFT 4-parameter safeTransferFrom bypass breaks lock withdrawal invariant, permanently freezing locked tokens
claim: The LockNFT contract overrides only the 3-parameter safeTransferFrom(address,address,uint256) to sync lock withdrawal addresses with NFT transfers, but fails to override the 4-parameter safeTransferFrom(address,address,uint256,bytes). The inherited ERC721Upgradeable 4-parameter implementation transfers the NFT without calling lockToken.transferLocks(), breaking the invariant that the NFT owner equals the lock withdrawal address. This causes the locked tokens to become permanently stuck—neither the old nor new NFT owner can withdraw them.
impact: Any user or marketplace that calls the 4-parameter safeTransferFrom (the standard ERC721 safe transfer with data) will break the lock, permanently freezing the underlying ERC20 tokens or NFT LP position. Only the LockToken owner can recover via recoverAssets(). Since many NFT protocols and marketplaces use the 4-parameter interface by default, this can happen inadvertently at scale, causing irreversible loss of user funds without owner intervention.

paths.trigger: Any caller invokes safeTransferFrom(from, to, tokenId, data) directly on the LockNFT proxy contract (4-parameter variant with bytes data). This is the canonical ERC721 safe transfer interface.
paths.exploit: Step 1: Attacker identifies a LockNFT token (deposit ID) that has nftMinted[_id] == true. Step 2: Attacker calls LockNFT.safeTransferFrom(originalOwner, attackerAddress, tokenId, '0x') — the 4-parameter overload. Step 3: The inherited ERC721Upgradeable implementation executes _isApprovedOrOwner check and _safeTransfer, transferring the NFT to the attacker. Step 4: lockToken.transferLocks() is never called, so the LockToken contract's lockedNFT[_id].withdrawalAddress still points to the original owner. Step 5: The attacker now owns the NFT but cannot withdraw the underlying locked tokens because withdrawTokens() requires _msgSender() == lockedNFT.withdrawalAddress. Step 6: The original owner also cannot withdraw because withdrawTokens() requires the caller to own the NFT (IERC721Extended(NFT).ownerOf(_id) == _msgSender()). Result: locked tokens are permanently frozen.
paths.state_after: The NFT has a new owner (attacker or accidental recipient), but the LockToken lockedNFTs[_id].withdrawalAddress still references the old owner. Neither party can call withdrawTokens() successfully because both conditions (NFT ownership AND withdrawal address match) cannot be simultaneously satisfied.
